g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Management. Today we'll explore how operating systems manage programs in execution.
A process is a program in execution - an active entity with a program counter and resources, unlike a passive program file.
g.text: A program is a passive file on disk.
A process is an active entity in memory.

g.data-narration: Understanding the difference: a program is static code, while a process is dynamic with memory, registers, and state.
A process has five key components: text (code), data (globals), stack (temporary data), heap (dynamic memory), and PCB.
g.text: Stack grows down, Heap grows up

g.data-narration: A process moves through five states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, and Terminated based on system events.
The Process Control Block is the data structure that stores all information about a process - its identity, state, and resources.
g.text: Each process has exactly ONE PCB containing all process information.

g.data-narration: The OS uses three types of queues: Job Queue (all processes), Ready Queue (CPU-bound), and Device Queues (I/O-bound).
A context switch saves one process state to PCB and loads another - pure overhead doing no useful work for the CPU.
g.text: Time depends on hardware: some CPUs have multiple register sets to reduce context switch time.

g.data-narration: fork() creates a child process - returns 0 to child and child's PID to parent. exec() then replaces the child's memory.
g.text: execlp() loads new program into process memory
Child's address space is completely replaced
Parent waits for child to finish

g.data-narration: Process termination: normal exit, error, or killed. Zombie has parent not calling wait(); orphan is adopted by init.
g.text: Terminated but parent hasn't called wait()

g.data-narration: Shared Memory IPC: processes share a memory region - fast but requires synchronization like semaphores to prevent race conditions.
g.text: No system calls for data access

g.data-narration: Message Passing: send() and receive() operations through OS - slower but easier to implement, works in distributed systems.
Key points: Process is active vs program passive. PCB stores all process info. fork returns 0 to child, PID to parent.
For exams: memorize the state diagram, PCB fields, fork() return values, zombie vs orphan definitions, and IPC comparison.
